322·6444 
322·5660 
32~·5901 
November 7, 1983 
Re: 
Your Request for written 
Advice, Our No. A-83-230 
You have requested our written advice with respect to 
possible conflicts of interest on the part of various members of 
the Lodi Economic Development Commission (LEDC). 
The nature, 
makeup and function of the LEDC are set forth in Resolution 
No. 82-4 of the Lodi City Council, a copy of which is attached. 
In general, the LEDC's purpose is to assist and advise the 
City Council in attracting new industry and in retaining 
existing industry. 
By Resolution No. 82-4, the makeup of the 
LEDC shall consist of one member from each of the following 
eight categories: 
(1) 
Commercial (Business Person) 
(2) 
Development 
(3) 
Real Estate 
(4) 
Financial 
(5) 
Architecture/Engineering 
(6) 
Accountancy 
(7) 
Indus tr ial 
(8) 
A Representative of the San Joaquin County Economic 
Development Agency 
Resolution No. 82-4, p.2. 


Ronald M. Stein 
November 7, 1983 
Page 2 
Your question relates to the fact that a business entity 
which is attracted to Lodi through the efforts of the LEDC 
and/or certain of its members may subsequently do business with 
an LEDC member or the member's firm. 
You have postulated that a 
business entity might purchase property from the real estate 
sector member or have architectural or engineering work 
performed by the member representing that sector. 
You have 
asked whether such circumstances raise the possibility for a 
conflict of interest on the part of members of the LEDC. 
The Political Reform Act..!.! (the "Act") requires that 
public officialsl/ disqualify themselves from making, 
participating in making, or using their official positions to 
influence the making of any governmental decision in which they 
have a financial interest. 
Section 87100. 
A financial interest 
is defined in Section 87103 as follows: 
An official has a financial interest in a decision 
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the decision will have a material 
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on 
the public generally, on: 
(a) 
Any business entity in which the public 
official has a direct or indirect investment worth more 
than one thousand dollars ($1,000). 
(b) 
Any real property in which the public 
official has a direct or indirect interest worth more 
than one thousand dollars ($1,000). 
(c) 
Any source of income, other than loans by a 
commercial lending institution in the regular course of 
business on terms available to the public without 
regard to official status, aggregating two hundred 
fifty dollars ($250) or more in value provided to, 
received by or promised to the public official within 
12 months prior to the time when the decision is made. 
l/ Government Code Sections 81000-91014. 
All statutory 
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise 
specified. 
~/ The members of the LEDC are public officials within 
the meaning of the Act; they are covered by Lodi's Conflict of 
Interest Code. 
Sections 82019(c) and 83048. 


Ronald M. Stein 
November 7, 1983 
Page 3 
(d) 
Any business entity in which the public 
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, 
employee, or holds any position of management. 
For purposes of this section, indirect investment 
or interest means any investment or interest owned by 
the spouse or dependent child of a public official, by 
an agent on behalf of a public official, or by a 
business entity or trust in which the official, the 
official's agents, spouse, and dependent children own 
directly, indirectly, or beneficially a 10-percent 
interest or greater. 
An architect will have a financial interest in his or her 
architectural firm because the firm will either be an employer, 
a source of income, and/or will constitute an investment, of the 
architect. 
Consequently, when acting in his/her official 
capacity as a member of the LEDC, an architect may not make, 
participate in making, or use his/her official position to 
influence the making of any governmental (LEDC) decision which 
will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect 
upon his or her architectural firm. 
Therefore, it would be improper for a member of the LEDC to 
use his or her official position to foster a private business 
relationship with a business entity which was being courted by 
the LEDC or the City of Lodi.l/ 
Likewise, it would be 
improper for an LEDC member to attempt to influence or in any 
way participate in a decision which involves bringing a company 
to Lodi where that decision will foreseeably result in a 
material financial effect upon the member's financial 
interest.i/ 
l/ For instance, a real estate broker/member of the LEDC 
cannot, in the course of LEDC recruitment of a company, discuss 
with the company its purchase of a parcel which he or she is 
trying to sell in the City of Lodi. 
i/ In addition, an LEDC member who is a real estate 
broker receives income from his or her firm for the purpose of 
selling property. 
Thus, he or she would also be prohibited from 
participating in LEDC decisions which will reasonably and 
foreseeably result in a sale of property by the firm, because 
there is a "nexus" between the governmental (LEDC) decision and 
the purpose for which the official receives income. 
2 Cal. Adm. 
Code Section 18702 (b) (3) (B). 
For a more detailed analysis of 
this issue, please see the enclosed copy of our advice letter to 
Linda Best, No. A-81-032. 


Ronald M. Stein 
November 7, 1983 
Page 4 
However, in the instance where the governmental decisions as 
to which companies to recruit and the actual recruitment 
activities have already been accomplished by the LEDC the 
company may in a decision which is independent from the decision 
to locate in Lodi, elect to utilize an LEDC member's services or 
to purchase his or her property. 
There would be nothing 
improper in such a case since the company's decision is private, 
not governmental. 
Should you have specific circumstances as to which your 
clients desire specific advice, please do not hesitate to 
contact us again. 
If you have questions regarding this letter, 
I may be reached by telephone at (916) 322-5901.

RESOLU'I'ION NO. 82- 4 
A RESOLUTION OF THE CITY COUNCIL OF THE 
CITY OF LODI ESTABLISHING AN ECONOMIC 
DEVELOPMENT COM.i"HSSION IN THE CITY OF LODI 
WHEREAS, the City Council of the City of Lodi finds 
that it is necessary and essential that the City use all 
practical means and measures to promote and enhance eco-
nomic development and increase opportunities for useful 
employment within the City of Lodi; and 
1;"lHEREAS, the City Council of the City of Lodi further 
finds that it is in the City's best interest to seek out 
industrial and commercial enterprises with the intent of 
having said industrial and commercial enterprises locate or 
relocate within the City of Lodi in order to promote and 
enhance the economic development and increase opportunities 
for useful employment within the City of Lodi: 
WHEREAS, the City Council of the City of Lodi is 
desirous of maintaining and encouraging existing industry 
within the City of Lodi. 
NOW, THEREFORE, in order to promote and enhance the 
economic development and increase opportunities for useful 
employment within the City of Lodi, the City Council of the 
City of Lodi hereby establishes an Economic Development 
Commission. 
The Commission shall consist of eight members to be 
appointed by the Mayor with the consent of the City Council. 
Said mb~bers shall serve at the pleasure of the City Council, 
and may be removed from office without cause upon an affirma-
tive vote of three (3) Councilmembers. 
EXHIBIT "A" 
-,-


( ( 
( 
( 
At least one representative of the following vocational 
categories shall be selected for mo~bership on the Economic 
Development Co~~ission: 
(1) 
COIT@ercial (Business person) 
(2) 
Development 
(3) 
Real Estate 
(4) 
Financial 
(5) 
Architecture/Engineering 
(6) 
Accountancy 
(7) 
Industrial 
(8) 
A representative of the San Joaquin County 
Economic Development Agency 
Members of the Economic Development Commission shall 
serve without compensation. 
All members of the Commission 
shall be entitled to such expenses as approved by the City 
Council. 
The terms of the office of the members of the Economic 
Development Commission shall be four years from the respective 
date of appointment, provided however, that terms of office of 
the various members of the Economic Development Commission 
adopted after the effective date of this Resolution shall be 
determined by lot so that there will be staggered terms with 
one member to serve one year, two members to serve two years, 
two members to serve three years, and two members to serve 
four years. 
The representative from the San Joaquin County 
Economic Development Agency shall serve for the same term as 
said representative serves as a representative to the San 
Joaquin County Economic Development Agency. 
-2-


( 
( 
(~ 
Any member appointed to fill a vacancy occuring prior 
to the expiration of the term for which the predecessor was 
appointed shall be appointed for the remainder of such term. 
There shall be no restriction on the number of consecu-
tive terms an individual may serve subject to their reappont-
ment by the City Council. 
The Economic Development Commission shall have powers 
to elect its own presiding officer with the title of Chair-
person, and such other officers as may be necessary or expedient 
and to adopt rules of order and procedure. 
The Economic Development Commission shall meet at the call 
of the Chairperson or majority of the Commission, but not less 
than one time every month. 
A majority of the members of the 
Economic Development Commission shall constitute a quorumi and 
a majority of the quorum is sufficient for the Commission to 
take action. 
The Commission shall keep an accurate record of 
its proceedings and transactions. 
The deliberations of the Economic Development Commission 
shall be held in public session, but this shall not constitute 
the sessions as public hearings as a matter of right, open to 
the participation by members of the general public at large, 
except at the will of the majority of the Economic Development 
Commission. 
-3-


( ( 
( 
The City of Lodi shall furnish to the Economic Development 
Commission adequate secretarial assistance, professional 
support staff, and meeting space, contingent upon the avail-
ability of funds. 
Upon the request of the Economic Development 
Commission, the City shall also make available the services and 
advice of various administrative departments. 
DUTIES: 
(1) 
The Economic Development Commission shall collect 
information on opportunities for business and 
investment in the City of Lodi. 
(2) 
Jhe Economic Development Commission shall prepare 
pamphlets explaining opportunities within the City. 
(3) 
The Economic Development Commission shall prepare 
pamphlets outlining City regulations affecting 
businesses. 
(4) 
The Economic Development Commission shall seek out 
Commercial and industrial businesses for the City of 
Lodi. 
(5) 
The Economic Development Commission shall recommend 
and advise the City Council on City priorities in 
the area of economic development through a comprehensive 
set of goals, objectives, and strategies. 
(6) 
The Economic Development Commission shall identify 
and define critical economic development issues, 
problems, opportunities and constraints. 
(7) 
The Economic Development Commission shall act as 
an advisory body to the Lodi City Council on key 
economic development issues. 
A 
v 


( 
( 
( 
(8) 
The Economic Development Commission shall assist 
and facilitate the coordination of local economic 
d0Jelopment efforts on those organizations currently 
involved in the economic development activities. 
{9} 
The Economic Development Commission shall facilitate 
public and private sector coordination in the develop-
ment of specific programs and projects. 
DATED: January 20, 1982 
I hereby certify that Resolution No. 82-4 was passed and 
adopted by the City Council of the City of Lodi in a 
regular meeting held January 20, 1982 by the following 
vote: 
Ayes: 
Noes: 
Councilmen - Katnich, Hughes, Pinkerton, and 
McCarty 
Councilmen 
None 
Absent: 
Councilmen - Murphy

sections.question: Is there a conflict of interest in violation of Section 81000 if the members of the Economic Development Commission (an advisory group of the City Council of the City of Lodi) perform in the functions of their respective capacities below: In representing the City in an outreach program to attempt to have businesses and industries locate within the City of Lodi, they represent to said businesses or industries that are members of the Economic Development Commission, and upon the business or industry deciding to locate in Lodi, a member then proceeds to sell a parcel of land for the location of the business or industry which the member listed as a real estate broker of the Commission?
sections.conclusion: Therefore, it would be improper for a member of the LEDC to use his or her official position to foster a private business relationship with a business entity which was being courted by the LEDC or the City of Lodi. Likewise, it would be improper for an LEDC member to attempt to influence or in any way participate in a decision which involves bringing a company to Lodi where that decision will foreseeably result in a material financial effect upon the member's financial interest. However, in the instance where the governmental decisions as to which companies to recruit and the actual recruitment activities have already been accomplished by the LEDC the company may in a decision which is independent from the decision to locate in Lodi, elect to utilize an LEDC member's services or to purchase his or her property. There would be nothing improper in such a case since the company's decision is private, not governmental.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether members of the Lodi Economic Development Commission violate conflict of interest laws when they participate in recruiting businesses to the city and then subsequently conduct private business transactions (such as real estate sales) with those same businesses.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: LEDC members cannot use their official positions to solicit private business from companies being recruited by the commission, as this violates conflict of interest law. However, once recruitment decisions are finalized and the company independently chooses to conduct business with an LEDC member in a separate transaction unrelated to the recruitment, this is permissible as it is a private decision rather than a governmental one.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document includes both incoming request (dated October 6, 1983) and FPPC response letter (dated November 7, 1983). Question and conclusion sections clearly identifiable. Minor OCR artifacts present in incoming request portion but did not significantly impact extraction. Footnotes provide additional regulatory context.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Is there a conflict of interest in violation of Section 81000 if the members of the Economic Development Commission (an advisory group of the City Council of the City of Lodi) perform in the functions of their respective capacities below: In representing the City in an outreach program to attempt to have businesses and industries locate within the City of Lodi, they represent to said businesses or industries that are members of the Economic Development Commission, and upon the business or industry deciding to locate in Lodi, a member then proceeds to sell a parcel of land for the location of the business or industry which the member listed as a real estate broker of the Commission?

CONCLUSION: Therefore, it would be improper for a member of the LEDC to use his or her official position to foster a private business relationship with a business entity which was being courted by the LEDC or the City of Lodi. Likewise, it would be improper for an LEDC member to attempt to influence or in any way participate in a decision which involves bringing a company to Lodi where that decision will foreseeably result in a material financial effect upon the member's financial interest. However, in the instance where the governmental decisions as to which companies to recruit and the actual recruitment activities have already been accomplished by the LEDC the company may in a decision which is independent from the decision to locate in Lodi, elect to utilize an LEDC member's services or to purchase his or her property. There would be nothing improper in such a case since the company's decision is private, not governmental.

Section 87100. A financial interest is defined in Section 87103 as follows: An official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on: (a) Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment worth more than one thousand dollars ($1,000). (b) Any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth more than one thousand dollars ($1,000). All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise specified. ~/
embedding.summary: The FPPC advises the City of Lodi on conflict of interest issues arising from the Lodi Economic Development Commission, whose members represent various business sectors. The Commission addresses whether LEDC members can conduct private business transactions with companies that the Commission helped recruit to the city.
